title: CCI Cost Intelligence Demo
subtitle: Full product walkthrough — Snapshot, Opportunities, Procedure Detail

rules: We are testing the product, not you. There are no wrong answers.
Please think out loud as you go — tell me what you are looking at and what you are thinking.
If you get stuck, tell me what you expected to happen.
I may stay quiet for a moment — that is intentional, not a sign you did anything wrong.
setup.noHelpPolicy: Wait 10 seconds before prompting. If stuck, ask: What would you do next if I was not here? Do not point to UI elements or explain what things do. If they ask is this right — say: What do you think?

groups.tabContext: Participant has not seen the product yet. No prototype on screen.

questions.label: Current workflow
questions.mainQuestion: Walk me through how you currently identify which cost opportunities are worth pursuing. How do you decide where to focus first?
probingQuestions: What does that process look like week to week?
Who else is involved in that decision?
What data do you rely on most?
questions.whatToDo: Let them answer fully without interrupting. Do not mention the product or any features.
questions.whatToListenFor: What triggers their attention — volume, variance, physician outliers, service line? What data sources do they trust or distrust?

questions.label: Pain in current process
questions.mainQuestion: What is the most frustrating part of that process right now?
probingQuestions: How much time does that take you?
Has that caused you to miss something important before?
If that problem went away, what would change for you?
questions.whatToDo: Stay curious and neutral. Do not reassure them that the product solves this — yet.
questions.whatToListenFor: Time waste, data gaps, lack of trust in numbers, difficulty explaining to stakeholders. These are the pain points the product should address.

groups.tabContext: Navigate to the Snapshot tab. Allow the participant to orient themselves before asking anything.

questions.label: First impression
questions.mainQuestion: Take a moment and look at this screen. Tell me what you are seeing.
probingQuestions: What stands out to you first?
What do you think this screen is for?
Is there anything here you would want to click on?
questions.whatToDo: Do not explain anything. Give them at least 20 seconds of silence first. Watch where their eyes go.
questions.whatToListenFor: Do they read the numbers or the labels first? Do they notice the left panel? Do they gravitate toward a specific card or number?

questions.label: Finding a focus area
questions.mainQuestion: Based on what you can see here, where would you start if you were looking for your biggest cost opportunity?
probingQuestions: What made you pick that one?
Is there anything here that would change your mind?
What would you need to see to feel confident about that choice?
questions.whatToDo: Let them navigate freely. Resist the urge to guide them. Note what they click and what they skip.
questions.whatToListenFor: Do they use the sort tabs? Do they read the opportunity cards? Do they look at the left panel at all? Any confusion about what a number means?

questions.label: Left panel usefulness
questions.mainQuestion: What do you make of the information on the left side of the screen?
probingQuestions: Is that information useful to you in this context?
When would you look at that?
Is anything missing from it?
questions.whatToDo: Point broadly to the left panel area without naming any specific element.
questions.whatToListenFor: Do they understand what the filters or summary metrics represent? Is the density too high or too low? Do they see it as context or noise?

groups.tabContext: Navigate to the Opportunities tab. Let them land on it and look before asking.

questions.label: Scanning the list
questions.mainQuestion: Looking at the list of opportunities in front of you — how would you decide which ones deserve your attention?
probingQuestions: What column or number would you look at first?
Is the way this is sorted useful to you?
Is there a way you would want to group or filter this?
questions.whatToDo: Do not mention sorting or filtering unless they bring it up. Watch what they scroll past and what they pause on.
questions.whatToListenFor: Do they prioritize by dollar value, volume, or something else? Are they looking for physician names? Do they want to see service line or department grouping?

questions.label: Acting on an opportunity
questions.mainQuestion: Pick one opportunity from this list that you would want to act on. Tell me why you chose that one.
probingQuestions: What would your next step be after picking this?
Who would you bring this to?
What would you need before you could take that to your CMO or CFO?
questions.whatToDo: Let them choose without guidance. If they hesitate, say: There is no wrong answer — just whatever feels most relevant to you.
questions.whatToListenFor: What justifies action in their mind? Is it size of opportunity, confidence in the data, physician relationship, or something else entirely?

questions.label: Missing information
questions.mainQuestion: Is there anything you would want to see on this page that is not here?
probingQuestions: If you could add one column to this list, what would it be?
Is there a view you wish you could switch to?
What would make this page more useful in your day to day work?
questions.whatToDo: This is a generative question — let them think out loud. Do not prompt with suggestions.
questions.whatToListenFor: Requests for payer mix, physician tenure, prior engagement history, benchmark comparisons, or trend over time.

groups.tabContext: Click into one of the opportunity cards to open the procedure detail view.

questions.label: Understanding the detail view
questions.mainQuestion: Tell me what you are looking at now. What is this screen showing you?
probingQuestions: Is that what you expected to see when you clicked in?
What does this tell you that the list view did not?
Is anything here surprising?
questions.whatToDo: Stay quiet for the first 15 seconds. Let them read and react before asking anything.
questions.whatToListenFor: Do they understand the physician breakdown immediately? Do they look for cost vs charge data? Do they understand the benchmark line?

questions.label: Physician variation
questions.mainQuestion: Looking at the physician data on this screen — what story does this tell you?
probingQuestions: Does the variation here seem meaningful to you?
What would you do with this information?
Would you feel comfortable sharing this with the physicians involved?
questions.whatToDo: Do not say the word outlier. Let them use their own vocabulary to describe what they see.
questions.whatToListenFor: How do they frame physician variation — as a performance issue, a data issue, or a workflow issue? Do they see this as actionable or politically sensitive?

questions.label: Readiness to act
questions.mainQuestion: If you walked out of this tool right now, do you feel like you have enough to take action on this opportunity?
probingQuestions: What would you still need?
Who would you share this with and in what format?
What would success look like 90 days from now?
questions.whatToDo: This is the key confidence-check question. Let them answer fully before probing.
questions.whatToListenFor: What gaps remain — is it data confidence, physician engagement process, executive sponsorship, or time? What format do they need to take it upstream?

groups.tabContext: Close or minimize the prototype. Closing questions only — no screen needed.

questions.label: Overall reaction
questions.mainQuestion: Stepping back from everything you just saw — what is your overall reaction?
probingQuestions: Was there a moment that felt particularly useful?
Was there a moment where you lost trust in what you were seeing?
Does this feel like something your team would actually use?
questions.whatToDo: Keep this open and unhurried. This is where the most honest feedback tends to come out.
questions.whatToListenFor: Overall sentiment, specific moments of delight or friction, and any signals about adoption barriers within their organization.

questions.label: One thing to change
questions.mainQuestion: If you could change one thing about what you saw today to make it more useful for your work, what would it be?
probingQuestions: Why is that the most important thing to you?
How would that change how you use it?
Is there anything else you would want us to know?
questions.whatToDo: Accept their answer without defending the product. Say: That is really helpful — thank you.
questions.whatToListenFor: The single biggest unmet need. This is the most important data point from the session.
